writing_task: I need help drafting a mid-year impact update for our major donors — something shorter than a full newsletter, maybe 300-400 words, that bridges the gap between our annual report and our fall fundraising appeal. The update centers on a workforce training program we run in partnership with community colleges, and I have a few specific stories from participants I want to weave in. Can you help me shape this into something that feels like a genuine check-in rather than a stealth ask?

drafts.rubric:
Dear [Name],

Six months ago, Marisol drove forty minutes each way to her first class at Eastside Community College — her certification books on the passenger seat, her shift at the warehouse ending at 10 p.m. the night before. She chose this. She registered, she showed up, and last month she accepted a healthcare technician position that doubled her hourly wage.

She wasn't alone in that decision. This year, 94 people enrolled in the workforce training program you helped build — choosing to move into healthcare, advanced manufacturing, and IT support roles that the region's employers have been unable to fill. Sixty-one have completed certification coursework. Forty-three are already in new positions. Those numbers are early, and we're watching closely, but the direction is clear.

Eastside and our two other partner colleges recruited and retained this cohort not because we handed them a model, but because their advisors already had relationships with students who needed exactly this pathway. What your investment made possible was the infrastructure — subsidized childcare coordination, transportation stipends, and employer partnerships — that let those existing relationships actually move people forward.

Darius, who graduated from Eastside's IT track in April, put it simply: "I'd already tried twice. This time I didn't have to choose between the class and keeping the lights on."

The decision you made to fund this model is producing a measurable result: working adults are completing credentials they couldn't finish before, and employers are hiring them. That's the bet, and it's playing out.

We're heading into fall with a waitlist of 34 people for the next cohort. Expanding to serve them fully requires $180,000 — enough to extend the support infrastructure through the second cohort cycle. We'll share a full ask in September, but we wanted you to see where things stand now.

Marisol is already mentoring one of the people on that waitlist. She didn't wait to be asked.

With gratitude and respect,

[Your name]
drafts.generic:
We wanted to reach out mid-year simply to say: the work is happening, and you are part of why.

This spring, our workforce training partnership with local community colleges served more than 120 participants across three cohorts. Among them was Darnell, who enrolled while working nights at a warehouse and completed his certification in logistics management in April. He started a new position two weeks later. There was also Maria, a single mother who told her cohort advisor that the program was "the first time anyone treated my time like it mattered." She graduates in August.

These stories do not happen in isolation. They happen because instructors have current curriculum, because wraparound support is funded, because someone believed the investment was worth making. That someone includes you.

We are on track to exceed our annual placement goal by roughly 15 percent, and we have already begun conversations with two additional college partners for next year. The momentum feels real, and we did not want to wait until fall to share it.

Thank you for staying in this with us.
drafts.preference:
Dear [Donor Name],

We wanted to pause midway through the year and share something we've been seeing up close in our workforce training program — the kind of progress that doesn't always make it into annual reports but that you made possible.

This spring, 47 participants completed our certificate program in partnership with [Community College Name]. More than that, 38 of them have already moved into new positions. Behind that number is someone like Maria, a single mother who spent three years in part-time retail work before enrolling. She finished her logistics certification in April and accepted a full-time role with benefits the following week. "I finally feel like I have a floor under me," she told us.

Or Jerome, who came to the program after a layoff at 52, convinced the job market had moved on without him. He graduated alongside students half his age and is now training others at a local manufacturing facility.

These aren't outliers. They're what the program looks like when it's working — and it's working because of the sustained investment you've made in it.

We're currently preparing to launch the fall cohort, and we have 60 participants ready to begin. Funding for training materials, wraparound support services, and employer partnership coordination runs approximately $1,200 per participant. If you're in a position to make a mid-year gift, even a partial contribution toward one participant's journey would go directly into the cohort starting in September.

You can give online at [website] or reach out to [contact name] directly at [email] — she'd love to hear from you and can answer any questions about where the program is headed next.

Thank you for staying in this with us. We'll be back in touch in the fall with a fuller update, but we didn't want to wait that long to say: it's going well, and you're a real part of why.

With gratitude,
[Name]
[Organization]

writing_task: I need to take the Marisol story further — we want to use it as the anchor for our fall fundraising appeal, but I'm not sure how to extend it without it feeling like we're just recycling the same narrative. Can you help me draft a 500-600 word appeal that picks up where her story is now (she completed her certification in May and just started a new job), and builds to a specific ask around funding the next cohort of women in our workforce program?

drafts.rubric:
Last May, Marisol walked into her certification exam carrying a folder she'd organized the night before — every practice test, every study sheet, color-coded by topic. She'd rearranged her work schedule, found someone to pick up her kids on exam day, and sat down at that table on her own terms. She passed on the first try.

This fall, she's three months into a new role in healthcare administration — a position she applied for, negotiated, and accepted. She commutes forty minutes each way and says she doesn't mind it.

"I finally feel like I'm building something," she said. "Not waiting for something."

Marisol wasn't the only one making that kind of decision last year. Fourteen women in our fall workforce cohort completed their certifications. Eleven are now in new roles. That's early data — we're honest about that — but it's consistent with what we saw in the pilot year, and it's pointing in the same direction.

The next cohort is scheduled to begin in January. We have eighteen women on the waitlist. We have confirmed capacity for twelve.

Your investment last year helped build the infrastructure that made Marisol's cohort possible — the employer partnerships, the childcare coordination, the curriculum that women in the program helped redesign. That wasn't a small bet. It produced a working model.

The decision in front of you now is whether to extend it.

Funding the full January cohort — all eighteen women — requires $47,000. At $1,500 per participant, each contribution directly determines how many seats open in January. Six women are currently positioned to join but unconfirmed because the funding isn't there yet.

The women on that waitlist have already done what Marisol did — researched the program, applied, arranged their schedules around an opportunity they chose to pursue. What happens next depends on whether we can meet them there.

If you're ready to continue this work with us, you can make your gift at [link] or by returning the enclosed envelope. If you'd like to talk through the model before deciding, reach out directly — we'd welcome that conversation.

Marisol asked us recently whether we had a waitlist. We told her yes. She nodded and said, "Make it work."

We're trying to.
drafts.generic:
This May, Marisol walked across a stage and accepted her certification — the piece of paper that had seemed impossible just eighteen months ago, when she first walked through our doors uncertain whether anyone would take a chance on her.

She started her new job three weeks later.

But here is what Marisol told us recently, sitting in the same room where she once attended her first workshop: "I keep thinking about the woman who comes after me. I want her to have this too."

That woman exists. In fact, there are fourteen of them — fourteen women who have applied to our next workforce program cohort and are waiting to find out if we can say yes.

Whether we can say yes depends entirely on what happens in the next six weeks.

Our fall fundraising goal is $47,000 — the exact amount needed to fund the full cohort through certification, job placement support, and the wraparound services that make the difference between a woman who completes the program and one who has to drop out because life got in the way. Childcare stipends. Transportation assistance. The small emergencies that derail big dreams.

Marisol needed those things. Most women in our program do.

When you give today, you are not funding a statistic. You are funding the specific, unglamorous, transformative work of helping a woman rebuild her economic life — the Tuesday night study sessions, the mock interviews, the moment someone finally believes the job offer is real.

Marisol's story did not end in May. It is just beginning. And fourteen more stories are waiting to start.

Will you help us open that door? A gift of $250 sponsors one week of wraparound support. A gift of $1,000 covers a full certification exam and materials. Every dollar moves us closer to saying yes to every woman on that waiting list.

Give before December 1st, and your gift will be matched dollar for dollar by a generous board member who believes, as Marisol does, that the woman who comes next deserves the same chance.

Donate at [link], or return the enclosed envelope today. Marisol is rooting for you — and so are we.
drafts.preference:
Dear Friend,

In May, Marisol walked across a small stage in a community center conference room and accepted her healthcare administration certificate. Her daughter was in the third row, filming it on her phone. Marisol told us later that she kept thinking about all the Tuesday evenings she had driven straight from her shift to class, exhausted, sometimes eating dinner in the car. She thought about almost quitting in February. She didn't quit.

This September, she started a new position as a medical office coordinator — her first full-time job with benefits in eleven years. The first time she saw her insurance card, she cried.

That is the whole story, really. Not a dramatic rescue or a sudden transformation, but a woman deciding, over and over again across nine months, to keep going. Our workforce program exists to make that kind of persistence possible — to fill in the gaps that make it too easy to stop: the childcare costs, the transportation barriers, the moments when someone needs to hear that they are close, just keep going.

Last year, 34 women completed our program. Of those, 29 are currently employed in their field of training, at an average starting wage nearly double what they were earning before. Those numbers matter to us, but what they represent matters more — 34 families with a little more breathing room, 34 women who built something they can keep.

We are now enrolling our fall cohort, and we have 12 women ready to begin. They have already done the hard part of showing up and saying yes. What we need now is the funding to say yes back to them.

A gift of $150 covers one month of transportation support for a participant. $500 funds the certification exam fees that are often the final hurdle between a woman and her credential. $1,500 sponsors a full participant through the program — from orientation to job placement. Every amount moves us closer to being able to open the door to all 12 women who are waiting.

If Marisol's story stays with you, the most direct thing you can do is make a gift today at [giving link] or return the enclosed reply card. Your contribution to this fall cohort means that a year from now, there will be another daughter in the third row, filming.

With gratitude,

[Name]
[Organization]

The urgency framing ('depends entirely on what happens in the next six weeks') tips toward manipulation. And 'helping a woman rebuild her economic life' puts the org back in the helper seat.

writing_task: Okay, so the fall appeal is basically locked — really happy with where that landed. Now I need to write the accompanying email to our mid-level donors ($500–$2,500 range) that goes out the same week, and I want it to reference the Marisol story without just copy-pasting the whole thing. Can you help me draft something that feels like a companion piece rather than a repeat?

drafts.rubric:
Subject: She already knew what she needed

Marisol had already mapped out her next step before she walked into her first session. She just needed the room to work it out loud.

You made that room possible. When you invested last year, you weren't responding to a gap — you were backing a model built on the premise that people arrive with their own momentum. Marisol is one of 47 participants this cohort who did exactly that.

We have 23 people on the waitlist for spring. Your decision to renew at any level between $500 and $2,500 directly determines how many of them get that same room.

There's someone on that list who already knows what she needs. She's waiting to find out whether the resources will be there when she arrives.
drafts.generic:
Subject: You made Marisol's story possible

Dear [Name],

If you've seen our fall appeal, you've met Marisol. But here's what we couldn't fit on the page: behind every story like hers is a community of steady, committed supporters — people exactly like you.

Your giving at this level makes the difference between a program that survives and one that truly thrives. Marisol's outcome wasn't luck. It was the result of consistent investment from donors who show up year after year.

As we close out this season, we're asking you to continue that commitment. Your gift today keeps the next Marisol's story from going unwritten.

With gratitude,
[Signature]
drafts.preference:
Dear [First Name],

A few weeks ago, we shared Marisol's story in our fall appeal. If you read it, you know she's someone who stays with you.

What we didn't have room to say is what happened next. After Marisol got back on her feet, she started volunteering with us on Wednesday mornings. She brings donuts. She knows everyone's name. That part — the turning around and reaching back — is what your giving actually makes possible.

Last year, donors at your level helped 47 families move from crisis to stability. You're a meaningful part of that.

Would you consider a gift of [amount] this fall? You can give at [link]. Thank you, genuinely.

user_reason: Draft A is the clear winner for me. It does everything I care about without announcing that it's doing it. Marisol is the subject with her own momentum and plan — she 'mapped out her next step,' she 'knew what she needed.' The donor is framed as a strategic investor who backed a model, not a generous soul who helped a poor woman. The ask is surgical: 23 waitlist spots, $500–$2,500, direct causation. And that closing line — 'She's waiting to find out whether the resources will be there' — creates urgency without guilt-tripping. Nobody is suffering for your inaction; someone capable is simply waiting for a room. Draft C is second. The detail about Marisol bringing donuts and knowing everyone's name is exactly the kind of specific human texture I love, and her volunteering reframes her as someone with agency who gives back — not a recipient. But it stumbles: 'got back on her feet' is vague, the number comes before any story context, and 'thank you, genuinely' reads a little performative. The ask is also generic. Draft B is a dealbreaker for me. 'The next Marisol's story from going unwritten' is the kind of line that sounds moving but actually centers the non-profit as narrator and gatekeeper of other people's stories. 'People exactly like you' is flattery, not strategy. And 'thrives vs. survives' is the vague impact claim I specifically want to avoid.

writing_task: Hey, I think we're in a good rhythm here. Can you help me draft a year-end appeal letter? Same program, but this one needs to center a different participant — I'm thinking someone from the agricultural cooperative cohort we ran last fall, a woman named Dora who expanded her farm operation and hired two neighbors. Same general feel as the Marisol piece, but the ask at the end should tie specifically to what additional funding would do for the next cohort.

drafts.rubric:
Dear [Donor Name],

Last October, Dora spread a hand-drawn expansion map across her kitchen table — three new grow beds, a cold storage unit, two neighbors she planned to bring on as paid workers — and enrolled in our agricultural cooperative cohort.

Six months later, she signed vendor contracts with two regional grocers, hired both neighbors, and filed paperwork to register her operation as an LLC.

She wasn't alone. Fourteen women in that cohort completed their business plans. Nine secured outside revenue by spring. Four hired staff.

Your investment in that cohort bet on a model: give producers the financial planning tools and cooperative structure to scale what they've already built. It worked.

We're opening applications for the next cohort now. Twenty-two farmers have already submitted interest forms. We have confirmed capacity for twelve.

Your gift this month decides how many of those twelve enroll, complete their cooperative agreements, and sign their first wholesale contracts by June. At $1,500, one participant moves through the full program — soil-to-shelf analysis, cooperative bylaws, and a live pitch to regional buyers. At $5,000, three do.

Dora's two neighbors are working. The next cohort is ready to start. The decision is yours.

[Signature]
drafts.generic:
Dear Friend,

Last fall, Dora walked into our agricultural cooperative program with a struggling small farm and a stubborn belief that things could be different. By spring, she had expanded her operation, secured new market contracts, and hired two of her neighbors — people who needed steady work and found it because Dora found her footing.

Stories like hers are why we do this work. And they depend entirely on supporters like you.

Your year-end gift will help us fund our next cooperative cohort, putting the same tools, training, and community in the hands of more farmers ready to grow.

Please give today.
drafts.preference:
Dear Friend,

Last fall, Dora walked into our agricultural cooperative program with a small plot of land, a lot of determination, and not quite enough capital to grow the way she knew she could.

By the time the cohort wrapped up, she had expanded her operation, taken on two neighbors as hired hands, and started supplying a local market she'd had her eye on for years. Three livelihoods, rooted in one woman's vision and a little well-placed support.

That's what this program does. Not in theory — in practice, in Dora's fields, in her neighbors' paychecks.

The next cohort is ready. We have women like Dora waiting, with the same drive and the same need for a strong start. What we need now is the funding to meet them where they are.

A gift of any size before December 31st helps us bring the next group through — covering training, cooperative development support, and the small-business resources that make the difference between getting by and genuinely growing.

Will you make a gift today? You can give at [donation link] or by returning the enclosed envelope. Because of your support, the next Dora won't have to wait.

With gratitude,

[Your name]

user_reason: Draft A is the clear winner. It centers Dora's agency throughout — she spread the map, she enrolled, she signed contracts, she filed paperwork. The impact numbers follow the story exactly the way I want them to. The ask is specific and tied directly to what we just read: soil-to-shelf analysis, cooperative bylaws, buyer pitches, $1,500 per participant. And 'your investment bet on a model' frames the donor as a strategic thinker, not a charity giver. The closing line about Dora's neighbors is earned. Draft B has warmth but falls apart on almost every hidden preference — 'a little well-placed support' makes us the hero, the ask is completely vague ('a gift of any size'), 'the next Dora' is a bit reductive of Dora herself, and 'because of your support' is exactly the benefactor framing I try to avoid. Draft C is the weakest — 'Stories like hers are why we do this work' centers the organization, not Dora. 'Supporters like you' is generic virtue signaling. The ask is three words with no specifics. It reads like a template, not a letter.

writing_task: Hey! I'm working on a mid-year impact report for our major donors — same program, but I need to translate some of the agricultural cohort's outcomes into a 2-page narrative that bridges Dora's story with the broader numbers from this cycle. Can you help me draft something that keeps the same voice we landed on in the appeal letter?

drafts.rubric:
Dora had been selling tamales out of a cooler for three years before she enrolled in the spring agricultural cohort. She already knew her customers by name, knew which days they'd be at the park, knew exactly how much masa she needed to clear fifty dollars before noon. What she didn't have was the food handler's certification that would let her move from the park to a licensed market stall. So she registered for the exam, studied on Sunday evenings after her kids were down, and passed on the first try. Six weeks later, she signed a vendor contract with the Eastside Farmers Market and opened her stall on a Saturday in April.

She wasn't the only one. Across this cycle's agricultural cohort, 34 participants completed their food handler's certification, 27 signed vendor contracts with licensed markets or institutional buyers, and 19 launched their first registered sales operation. Eleven of those businesses reached consistent monthly revenue within 60 days of opening. These are mid-year numbers — the final cohort completes in September — and the curve is still climbing.

Your investment in this model produced those contract signings. The bet you placed on a peer-led training structure — one where experienced vendors like Dora become the trust anchors for newer participants — is what moved people from informal sales to licensed storefronts. That's the mechanism your funding activated.

Fourteen people on the current waitlist are ready to do what Dora did: pass the exam, sign the contract, open the stall. A commitment of $1,500 moves one person from that list into the fall cohort and through to a signed vendor agreement. At $10,000, seven vendors open stalls before the end of the year. The next cohort starts enrollment in October. The decision you make now determines who's setting up tables in November.
drafts.generic:
When Dora planted her first certified seedlings last spring, she wasn't just investing in a harvest. She was betting on herself — on a system that finally bet back.

Dora is one of 47 farmers who completed our agricultural cohort this cycle, and her story echoes across every county we serve. Collectively, this group increased average household income by 34 percent, reduced post-harvest loss by nearly half, and brought 312 acres into sustainable production for the first time.

These outcomes don't happen by accident. They happen because of donors like you, who understand that lasting change begins with one person, one season, one seed at a time.
drafts.preference:
Mid-Year Impact Report — Agricultural Cohort

When Dora joined our agricultural cohort in January, she had a small plot of land, a notebook full of questions, and a determination that was hard to miss. By June, she had harvested her first commercial yield, secured a buyer relationship with a regional distributor, and trained two neighbors in the soil preparation techniques she learned through the program. That is not a marketing angle. That is just what happened.

Dora is one of 47 participants who completed the agricultural cohort this cycle. Across that group, 38 established at least one new revenue stream from their farms, and collective household income increased by an average of 34 percent compared to the same period last year. Those numbers mean something because of what sits behind them — early mornings, difficult learning curves, and the kind of quiet persistence it takes to try something new when the stakes are your family's stability.

None of this happens without sustained investment. If Dora's story moved you, we hope you will consider making a mid-year gift to carry this work through the second half of the cycle. A contribution of any amount before August 15th helps us confirm funding for the next cohort before enrollment opens in September. You can give at the link below, or reach out to us directly — we would love to hear from you.

user_reason: Draft C is the clear winner. Dora has full agency throughout — she registered, she studied, she passed, she signed. The story comes first, then numbers as evidence of scale, and the numbers are specific and meaningful. The donor framing is exactly right: 'your investment in this model produced' and 'the bet you placed' treats them as a strategic investor, not a benefactor. The peer-led training mechanism is named, which makes the ask feel grounded. It's cut off at the end but what's there is strong. Draft B is solid — Dora's story has real agency ('she harvested,' 'she secured,' 'she trained'), the numbers are paired well with the human context, and the voice is warm without being sentimental. But the ask falls apart: 'a contribution of any amount' is vague and disconnected from Dora's story, and 'we hope you will consider' is weak. The donor framing also slips into benefactor territory. Draft A is the weakest. 'Donors like you' who 'understand' change is classic virtuous-benefactor framing. 'These outcomes don't happen by accident — they happen because of donors like you' centers the donor as hero in a feel-good way rather than as a strategic investor. 'One seed at a time' is the kind of soft inspirational close I actively avoid. Dora's agency is present but thinner, and the numbers feel listed rather than evidenced.

criteria_analysis.reasoning: The rubric-guided draft excels: Dora is the grammatical subject throughout with decisive transitive verbs (enrolled, passed, signed, opened), donors are credited as strategic actors, and the organization never appears as a subject. The generic draft slips into org-centered framing ('our agricultural cohort,' 'we serve,' 'donors like you who understand') and uses the sentimental 'betting on herself' construction. The original preference draft is strong with Dora as subject and decisive verbs (joined, harvested, secured, trained), but the closing pivots to 'we hope you will consider' and 'we would love to hear from you,' pulling the org back to center stage.
